What once felt smooth now needs to be smooth. I can’t enjoy a cool explosion or a cutscene transition without watching the framerate dip. I used to play first-party Sony titles on PS3 and early PS4 with no idea what FPS even was — and I had a blast.

Then I switched to PC, and just like that, I couldn’t enjoy anything under 60. FPS was the first term that made me feel like I had to be a technician just to enjoy games. And once that switch flipped, there was no going back.

 Related
5 reasons high FPS doesn't guarantee a great gaming experience

A lot of other factors are at play

4 I can’t stop thinking about CPU and GPU temperatures

Why are the fans blowing hot air, and why is the PC making noise?

I used to think temperatures were for the weather. Now, they’re a reason to panic mid-boss fight. GPU temp, CPU temp, junction temp — suddenly I’m a part-time hardware therapist, whispering affirmations to my rig so it doesn’t melt down. Not because I cheaped out on a cooler, but because now I know there is a number my temps shouldn’t go above, based on the money I spent. Can’t I just not look and enjoy the game?

HWiNFO lives on my taskbar, right next to Steam. Not because I want it there, but because I need to know if my PC is “running hot.” And for some reason, temps magically drop only when I start monitoring them — like it knows it’s being watched.

Back then, a console either ran the game or it didn’t. It made noise? So what? The PS4 could’ve doubled as a jet engine, and it still ran God of War just fine. Nobody told me about thermal paste or pump alignment like I was building a nuclear reactor. I wasn’t supposed to know this stuff. I just wanted to game. Now, if my CPU hits 85°C, I’m clutching my chest. I miss ignorance. It was simpler — and cooler.

 Related
5 cable management habits I learned that improve temperatures more than extra fans

Sometimes, cable management can do much more than extra fans on your PC

4

3 Tinkering with graphics settings killed my plug-and-play joy

Graphics settings were never supposed to stress me out

For the longest time, graphic settings weren’t even a concept to me. I was just grateful when a game ran. That was the real benchmark — not Ultra vs. Low, but "Does this boot or crash the second I press Play?" Most of the games I grew up with were knock-offs, sketchy ports, or just badly optimized. If they launched, and I could move the character, that was plenty. Frame rate? Who cares? Shadows? Didn’t even know you could turn them off.

But today? Oh, man. I’m knee-deep in optimization rabbit holes before I even hit New Game. Every release is an event — first Digital Foundry drops their settings guide, then it’s hours of me testing what combination gets me the best mix of sharpness, stability, and thermal sanity. It’s like assembling IKEA furniture with no manual and constant fear of stuttering. Honestly, the one thing I really miss from only having a PS4 is the simplicity — I popped in the disc, and the only setting I tweaked was to turn subtitles on, and that was it. No DLSS, no FSR, no texture streaming budgets. Just me, my controller, and a game that ran the way it was meant to. I didn’t know how easy I had it.

 Related
3 reasons why I use DLSS despite its flaws

The pros far outweigh the cons

2

2 Microtransactions have turned games into shopping malls

I miss when games gave rewards, not microtransactions

I remember when the idea of unlocking something in a game was thrilling. You did something cool, hit a milestone, or just kept grinding, and boom — a new costume, weapon, or stage popped up like a surprise gift. But now? The only thing that pops up is a store tab. Skins aren’t earned, they’re bought. Progression isn’t personal, it’s priced. And the worst part? It works. I’ve spent real money on fake things more times than I care to admit.

The moment I truly realized microtransactions had me in a chokehold was when I spent an hour debating whether a cosmetic battle pass skin was “worth it.” Not good. That’s not a conversation I should be having with myself at 2AM. I miss when the only currency I needed in games was patience, not premium coins. We’ve come a long way, but man, I’d be lying if I said I didn’t miss the days when completing a game’s hardest level gave you a reward, not a prompt to visit the store.

The only good microtransaction I’ve ever made, in my opinion, is buying the Fortnite battle pass in 2017 for Season 3. It’s been paying for itself ever since.

 Related
5 additional costs you're probably forgetting in a gaming platform

You might buy buy a new console or gaming PC, but with a new gaming platform come hidden costs and fees that will keep clawing into your pockets.

1 I miss playing games, not studying the meta

I used to try my best, now I google someone else's best

There was a time when I just played the game. Lost a round? I'd try again, do better, get smarter. I didn't need to learn about tier lists or weapon builds. I just played. It was raw, messy, and incredibly rewarding. But now, if I want to be even remotely competitive, I have to study spreadsheets, patch notes, and Reddit threads. What happened to the joy of figuring things out myself?

The meta has made it feel like there’s only one “right” way to win — and worse, that if I’m not doing it, I’m wasting my time. Every game is a lab now. I’m checking if my build has synergy. I’m wondering if the gun I like is still viable after the latest nerf. It's exhausting. And the irony is, I used to love the challenge of getting better. Now, I’m just learning how to copy better.

 Related
I spent 100 hours recreating my high school in Fortnite and here's how it went

It had been an age-old dream, and what started with the Source engine, ended in Unreal Editor for Fortnite, eight years later.

1

I miss when gaming just felt like gaming

I can't help but feel like the more I know, the less I just play.

Look, I realize that I'm mourning the loss of childhood as much as I am missing "simpler" times — when it was about the worlds we escaped into, not the spreadsheets we built around them. Somewhere along the way, it stopped being plug and play, and started feeling like prep and pray.

I get it — knowledge is power. And of course I want the latest and best hardware, and I will continue spending hours fretting about frames and temps, but I can't help but feel like the more I know, the less I just play. And maybe, just maybe, I was better off not knowing all this in the first place.

item.title: The RTX 5060 is the nail in the coffin for 8GB graphics cards
item.itunes:title: The RTX 5060 is the nail in the coffin for 8GB graphics cards
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
While this most recent GPU release cycle is still largely underway, we've reached the tail end of the notable cards in both AMD and Nvidia's lineups. The release of the flagships and mid-range has come and gone, and I don't think it'd be too early to start reflecting on how everything went. MSRP, QA, and stock conversations dominated the discourse for most people, and that's understandable, considering just how many people couldn't get their hands on a card in the first place.

One conversation that must be had is one about VRAM. Once again, Nvidia released multiple GPUs that sported 8 GB of VRAM, which on the surface doesn't sound notable, but upon further examination, it just seems like a way to cut cost and create planned obsolescence. Mid-range cards with 8 GB of VRAM are not long for this world, and I think this is the last generation we'll see them.

 Related
GPUs with 8GB VRAM shouldn't exist in 2025, but Nvidia didn't get the memo

Nvidia stays adamant while the industry moves on from 8GB VRAM GPUs

4

Why the VRAM conversation matters

Whether you like it or not, it's going to affect you

Source: Wikimedia Commons

VRAM is your graphics card's dedicated video memory buffer. It holds data that your GPU needs quick access to, like the high-resolution textures in your favorite games. If your card runs out of space in its memory buffer, it starts to use your system memory as overflow space. Games will often allocate more memory than they need, but when you actually start running out of VRAM, you'll notice extremely low framerates and high frametimes— telltale signs your card has reached its capacity.

You can get around this by lowering texture quality and utilizing things like MIP streaming, but that's only if your favorite games support it, And it's not a cure-all, by any means. There are some cases where newer games will just straight up refuse to boot on an 8 GB card, which is the first sign that 8 GB cards are truly not long for this world.

 Related
Here's what actually happens when your GPU runs out of VRAM

Everyone hates 8GB graphics cards. Here's why.

12

The 5060 is a shell of what the 60-series once was

Along with the 70-series, for that matter

Source: Pexels

Take a walk with me down memory lane for a moment. When the 3060 Ti launched in late-2020, it didn't get rave reviews, but it was considered one of the strongest mid-range GPUs we had seen to that point. It offered strong 1440p performance for a $399 MSRP card. You couldn't really get it for that price, but if you could get it anywhere in the ballpark, it was good value. It had similar performance in some scenarios to the RTX 2080, for a much lower price to boot.

Fast-forward to the current year, and the 5060 is being beaten or at least matched by that very same RTX 3060 Ti in many benchmarks at multiple resolutions. And this is before we even mention the 8 GB VRAM buffer that these cards still sport, 5 years later. The cracks are starting to show at 1080p, but at 1440p and above, there are few scenarios where these 60-class cards make sense as products in today's PC landscape. AMD isn't getting off scot-free either. Their 8 GB version of the 9060 XT is just as irrelevant as Nvidia's offering in the same price class.

 Related
5 reasons your GPU's VRAM capacity matters more than ever

Remember the fears about low VRAM on GPUs? They're all coming true

4

"The more you buy, the more you save"*

(*At least, that's what it looks like on the surface)

The RTX 5060 and 5060 Ti 8 GB don't make much sense as high-resolution gaming cards because of their VRAM buffer. As resolution increases, so does the size of textures and the potential size of your frame buffer. You could argue it's now an "esports" card, but there are plenty of last-gen options that run esports titles at several hundreds of frames per second. So if you want your new 60-class card to be usable beyond the next couple of years, you'll need to spend a little more. Fair enough, the 5060 Ti 16 GB is currently running at around $550, which is still well above its MSRP of $430 USD, but surely that's a decent deal, right?

Unfortunately, the 5060 Ti at $550 gets thrashed compared to last-generation cards that can be found in that price range. I can easily find an RTX 4070 Ti for around that price in my area. An RX 6950 XT can be found at well below that, and while it might struggle in RT, it's still more than serviceable in raster at well above 1080p. These cards have more than enough VRAM to handle themselves in newer titles as well. They have a sizable enough frame buffer to keep the GPU itself relevant for longer. The 5060 and 5060 Ti 8 GB don't have that, and neither does the 8 GB model of the 9060 XT. These are cards with great silicon on them, but they're being cut down to maintain a product class that only exists because of scarcity.

One thing last-gen cards don't have, is technologies like DLSS 4, and the bells and whistles that come along with them. Unfortunately for buyers of new 8 GB cards, these technologies do nothing to save them, because they rely on the VRAM buffer to work their magic.

 Related
DLSS is Nvidia's Achilles heel — here's why

It's great for gamers, but DLSS can only hurt Nvidia in the long run.

5

It's time we raise the VRAM floor

Nvidia has abandoned the mid-range, 1440p gamer on a budget, and it started with the release of the RTX 5090. The reason for the product segmentation we've seen for the RTX 50 series started at the top. Each card below the 5090 just feels like an upsell, and while AMD have offered a compelling option in the 9070 XT and 9060 XT 16 GB, those cards also come with their own quandaries, pricing and performance-wise. Cards with 8 GB of VRAM just don't cut it anymore, and I doubt we'll see them again.

item.title: These 6 scripts are a godsend for my Proxmox home lab
item.itunes:title: These 6 scripts are a godsend for my Proxmox home lab
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I’ve tried out numerous home server operating systems and virtualization platforms over the past year. While each distro has certain useful things to bring to the table, Proxmox remains the undisputed winner for home labs, and for good reason. It offers everything from high-availability clusters and Ceph storage to native ZFS support, TurnKey LXC templates, and SDN facilities – all without charging a dime.

However, community support is a large part of what makes Proxmox my favorite virtualization platform. Among the myriads of guides, templates, and toolkits created by the community, the Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts repository stands out as the best resource for managing your PVE server. Initially developed by brilliant developer tteck (who sadly passed away from cancer last year), Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts is an amazing repository that includes everything you'll need to kick-start your self-hosting journey.

While it’s famous for its rich set of LXC and virtual machine scripts, the repository also includes a handful of shell commands that can modify certain aspects of your PVE server.

 Related
10 Proxmox tools that can transform your entire experience

Even the all-powerful Proxmox needs some extra utilities to reach its full potential

4

1 Proxmox VE Post Install

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/post-pve-install.sh)"

The vanilla installation of Proxmox has a couple of minor quirks that get annoying over time. For instance, the virtualization platform nags you about the lack of a subscription every time you log into the server. Likewise, Proxmox has the enterprise repository enabled by default, and you’ll need to replace it with the community repo when you want to update the packages on your Proxmox node.

Thankfully, the Proxmox VE Post Install can complete all these steps in one fell swoop. All you have to do is paste the Post Install command inside the Shell and watch the script take care of those pesky issues. I run the script every time I perform a fresh installation of Proxmox on my home lab devices (and inside nested virtualization environments).

2 Proxmox Clean Orphaned LVM

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/clean-orphaned-lvm.sh)"

Over the course of my Proxmox journey, I’ve created and deleted several LXCs and virtual machines. However, simply deleting a virtual machine doesn’t free up all the storage used by it. If you forget to enable the option to clear the LVM data when removing a virtual guest, its virtual volumes can continue to occupy gigabytes of space on your storage drives.

The Proxmox Clean Orphaned LVM script comes in really handy - I can just run the script and wait while it clears out the extra storage volumes left behind by non-existent virtual guests.

3 PVE Privilege Converter

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/pve-privilege-converter.sh)"

Unprivileged containers are a better option for security-conscious home labbers who want to prevent privilege escalation risks. After all, if a hacker were to break into your privileged container, they would be able to access the host Proxmox environment with root-level permissions. However, there are times when you might want to go with a privileged container. Perhaps you want an easier way to bind network shares to your server. Or maybe you want a simple and straightforward method to enable GPU passthrough for your containerized services.

But once you’ve deployed a service inside an unprivileged container, converting it into a privileged model (or vice versa) isn’t as simple as toggling a checkbox. Instead, you’ll have to manually back up the container and restore it with the right access privilege. The PVE Privilege Converter script automates this entire process, and you can use it to switch between privileged and unprivileged modes as often as you want.

4 Proxmox VE Cron LXC Updater

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/cron-update-lxcs.sh)"

Cron is the job scheduler built into Linux distributions, and is by far one of the best ways to automate your Linux projects. While it can take a while to get accustomed to their syntax, Cron expressions are great when you want to run certain tasks repeatedly at fixed intervals – and you can even harness this neat utility in your Proxmox server using the Cron LXC Updater script.

As you may have already guessed from the name, running this script causes Proxmox to create a new Cron task that grabs the updated LXC images and uses them to redeploy your containers. By default, the script runs at 12 am every Sunday and pulls new images for all your containers. But I’ve configured mine to check for updates every month and excluded certain high-priority LXCs from the automation, as I don’t want to risk losing them due to a broken image. While we’re on the subject…

5 Proxmox Update Repositories

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/update-repo.sh)"

If you’ve used Linux in the past, you may have dealt with broken package repositories. Whether it’s due to invalid links, faulty packages, or other minor issues on your own machine, it's not uncommon to encounter errors when attempting to install or update your packages.

The same holds true for Proxmox, which is essentially Debian packaged with some neat features and wrapped inside a convenient web UI. As such, you can run the Proxmox Update Repositories script should you encounter any issues while working with the LXCs on your PVE server.

6 Proxmox VE LXC IP-Tag

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/add-lxc-iptag.sh)"

Managing the IP addresses of your virtual guests doesn’t sound too complicated when you’ve only got a handful of services deployed on your Proxmox machine. But once your army of LXCs starts to grow out of control, you’ll have a hard time keeping track of each container’s network details.

That’s where the LXC IP-Tag script becomes extremely useful. Once you execute the script in your Proxmox Shell, it will append the IP address as a tag on each LXC service, making it easier to manage your container suite.

Don't forget about the container LXCs

Aside from these useful commands, the repository also has a couple of other scripts that deserve a shout-out. There's the Proxmox VE Processor Microcode, which adds some useful bug fixes and performance patches for a wide range of CPUs, while LXC Filesystem Trim runs the fstrim command to get rid of unused blocks from your virtual guests.

Then you've got the rest of the container scripts, which can deploy applications inside LXC environments. While I use a majority of these scripts in my home lab, the one for Vaultwarden deserves a special mention. If you were to deploy the password manager conventionally, you'd have to go through a long process just to set up HTTPS access for the container's web UI. Meanwhile, the Proxmox VE Helper-Script for Vaultwarden automatically creates a self-signed certificate, so you can save your passwords without too much hassle.

 Related
4 reasons why I'll stick with Proxmox even though the free version of ESXi is back

Why switch to ESXi again when Proxmox is just that good?

6
item.description:
I’ve tried out numerous home server operating systems and virtualization platforms over the past year. While each distro has certain useful things to bring to the table, Proxmox remains the undisputed winner for home labs, and for good reason. It offers everything from high-availability clusters and Ceph storage to native ZFS support, TurnKey LXC templates, and SDN facilities – all without charging a dime.

However, community support is a large part of what makes Proxmox my favorite virtualization platform. Among the myriads of guides, templates, and toolkits created by the community, the Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts repository stands out as the best resource for managing your PVE server. Initially developed by brilliant developer tteck (who sadly passed away from cancer last year), Proxmox VE Helper-Scripts is an amazing repository that includes everything you'll need to kick-start your self-hosting journey.

While it’s famous for its rich set of LXC and virtual machine scripts, the repository also includes a handful of shell commands that can modify certain aspects of your PVE server.

 Related
10 Proxmox tools that can transform your entire experience

Even the all-powerful Proxmox needs some extra utilities to reach its full potential

4

1 Proxmox VE Post Install

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/post-pve-install.sh)"

The vanilla installation of Proxmox has a couple of minor quirks that get annoying over time. For instance, the virtualization platform nags you about the lack of a subscription every time you log into the server. Likewise, Proxmox has the enterprise repository enabled by default, and you’ll need to replace it with the community repo when you want to update the packages on your Proxmox node.

Thankfully, the Proxmox VE Post Install can complete all these steps in one fell swoop. All you have to do is paste the Post Install command inside the Shell and watch the script take care of those pesky issues. I run the script every time I perform a fresh installation of Proxmox on my home lab devices (and inside nested virtualization environments).

2 Proxmox Clean Orphaned LVM

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/clean-orphaned-lvm.sh)"

Over the course of my Proxmox journey, I’ve created and deleted several LXCs and virtual machines. However, simply deleting a virtual machine doesn’t free up all the storage used by it. If you forget to enable the option to clear the LVM data when removing a virtual guest, its virtual volumes can continue to occupy gigabytes of space on your storage drives.

The Proxmox Clean Orphaned LVM script comes in really handy - I can just run the script and wait while it clears out the extra storage volumes left behind by non-existent virtual guests.

3 PVE Privilege Converter

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/pve-privilege-converter.sh)"

Unprivileged containers are a better option for security-conscious home labbers who want to prevent privilege escalation risks. After all, if a hacker were to break into your privileged container, they would be able to access the host Proxmox environment with root-level permissions. However, there are times when you might want to go with a privileged container. Perhaps you want an easier way to bind network shares to your server. Or maybe you want a simple and straightforward method to enable GPU passthrough for your containerized services.

But once you’ve deployed a service inside an unprivileged container, converting it into a privileged model (or vice versa) isn’t as simple as toggling a checkbox. Instead, you’ll have to manually back up the container and restore it with the right access privilege. The PVE Privilege Converter script automates this entire process, and you can use it to switch between privileged and unprivileged modes as often as you want.

4 Proxmox VE Cron LXC Updater

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/cron-update-lxcs.sh)"

Cron is the job scheduler built into Linux distributions, and is by far one of the best ways to automate your Linux projects. While it can take a while to get accustomed to their syntax, Cron expressions are great when you want to run certain tasks repeatedly at fixed intervals – and you can even harness this neat utility in your Proxmox server using the Cron LXC Updater script.

As you may have already guessed from the name, running this script causes Proxmox to create a new Cron task that grabs the updated LXC images and uses them to redeploy your containers. By default, the script runs at 12 am every Sunday and pulls new images for all your containers. But I’ve configured mine to check for updates every month and excluded certain high-priority LXCs from the automation, as I don’t want to risk losing them due to a broken image. While we’re on the subject…

5 Proxmox Update Repositories

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/update-repo.sh)"

If you’ve used Linux in the past, you may have dealt with broken package repositories. Whether it’s due to invalid links, faulty packages, or other minor issues on your own machine, it's not uncommon to encounter errors when attempting to install or update your packages.

The same holds true for Proxmox, which is essentially Debian packaged with some neat features and wrapped inside a convenient web UI. As such, you can run the Proxmox Update Repositories script should you encounter any issues while working with the LXCs on your PVE server.

6 Proxmox VE LXC IP-Tag

bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/community-scripts/ProxmoxVE/main/tools/pve/add-lxc-iptag.sh)"

Managing the IP addresses of your virtual guests doesn’t sound too complicated when you’ve only got a handful of services deployed on your Proxmox machine. But once your army of LXCs starts to grow out of control, you’ll have a hard time keeping track of each container’s network details.

That’s where the LXC IP-Tag script becomes extremely useful. Once you execute the script in your Proxmox Shell, it will append the IP address as a tag on each LXC service, making it easier to manage your container suite.

Don't forget about the container LXCs

Aside from these useful commands, the repository also has a couple of other scripts that deserve a shout-out. There's the Proxmox VE Processor Microcode, which adds some useful bug fixes and performance patches for a wide range of CPUs, while LXC Filesystem Trim runs the fstrim command to get rid of unused blocks from your virtual guests.

Then you've got the rest of the container scripts, which can deploy applications inside LXC environments. While I use a majority of these scripts in my home lab, the one for Vaultwarden deserves a special mention. If you were to deploy the password manager conventionally, you'd have to go through a long process just to set up HTTPS access for the container's web UI. Meanwhile, the Proxmox VE Helper-Script for Vaultwarden automatically creates a self-signed certificate, so you can save your passwords without too much hassle.

 Related
4 reasons why I'll stick with Proxmox even though the free version of ESXi is back

Why switch to ESXi again when Proxmox is just that good?

6

item.title: This project lets you play rock, paper, scissors versus your Raspberry Pi, complete with hand gesture recognition
item.itunes:title: This project lets you play rock, paper, scissors versus your Raspberry Pi, complete with hand gesture recognition
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • This Raspberry Pi project uses AI to play rock, paper, scissors, with over 95% accuracy and low latency.
  • No need for external server - AI processing on Raspberry Pi leads to quicker results and cost savings.
  • Possibilities extend beyond the game: tech could assist those with accessibility needs, and practical applications await.

The best Raspberry Pi projects are a lot of fun to use, and they teach you something while you build them. If you've ever wondered about using AI to identify hand gestures but are unsure where to get started, why not challenge it to a game of rock, paper, scissors? Not only do you learn how to build an AI system that can detect what hand gesture you're throwing, but you can put your SBC in its place and beat it at a game or two. It knows what it did.

 Related
7 creative uses for Raspberry Pi you probably haven’t tried yet

Your Raspberry Pi is good for more than just a media center or retro game emulator

5

You, too, can play rock, paper, scissors against your Raspberry Pi 5

In a post on Hackster, tinkerer Citrus posted their new rock, paper, scissors-based SBC project. It uses the Raspberry Pi 5, a Raspberry Pi Touch Display, and the Raspberry Pi AI camera to make a system that can detect what hand sign you're making.

However, Citrus didn't want the Raspberry Pi to take what's on the screen and beam it over to an AI model on an external server to work out what's happening. Not only does it take a while for the results to come back, but it'd likely cost a fortune to process. The solution? Use all the AI processing hardware on the Raspberry Pi to churn through the data and get a response quicker at no additional cost.

The above video shows the game in action. It seems to be both speedy and accurate when detecting what hand sign you're throwing. Sure enough, Citrus confirms that the system works very well:

Experimental results show that the model performs excellently, with gesture classification accuracy and recall consistently above 95%. Furthermore, the system demonstrates robust performance across a wide range of environmental conditions, with extremely low latency and efficient real-time processing power, suitable for practical interactive applications such as the rock-paper-scissors game demonstrated. This work provides practical and innovative solutions for low-cost, efficient and robust hand gesture recognition for commercial applications in interactive entertainment, healthcare and smart home systems

While this is a fun project by itself, there are lots of ways you could apply this tech other than playing rock, paper, scissors. For instance, you could allow people with accessibility needs to control technology with a single hand gesture. Here's hoping people find cool ways to put this tech to use.

item.title: This cool 3D-printed rubber band-powered cartridge is perfect for miniature firing ranges
item.itunes:title: This cool 3D-printed rubber band-powered cartridge is perfect for miniature firing ranges
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • These 3D-printed rubber band cartridges can shoot projectiles with high velocity.
  • Use caution with these cartridges, as they can be dangerous and are not suitable for aiming at people.
  • Consider using the cartridges for target practice on inanimate objects rather than in competitive scenarios.

Whenever humanity invents a new thing, people get to work on figuring out how to use it to make it fling objects at really high speeds. I haven't really seen people achieve this goal with 3D printing, but today is the day that changes. Someone posted a rubber band-powered cartridge you can use to shoot little pellets around, but I wouldn't recommend firing it at people.

 Related
This Raspberry Pi project creates a floating image using a 150-year-old technique, and I love it

Oldie but a goodie.

5

This rubber band-powered cartridge can fire a mean shot

In a post on the 3D printing subreddit, user IdonthaveQuestions posted a video of their 3D printed cartridges. Loading them is easy; add a rubber band, put a pellet on it, push it down with a loader like it's a 21st-century musket, then turn it 90 degrees. It'll then keep the pellet in a primed position, which then fires when you tap on its underside hard enough.

So, the first thing you may think of when looking at this project is making these the perfect weapon in your ongoing projectile war with your rivals. However, it's best you don't actually point this thing at another living being, as they're more powerful than they look. In fact, the creator added a disclaimer that's mostly about not taking out an eyeball:

  • It's a prototype and it might be unstable.
  • Make sure there's no kids around. they might swallow the projectile.
  • DO NOT look into the hole of the loaded cartridge.
  • This Thing Hurts.
  • When loading, Twisting counter-clockwise must be done well. (It might launch itself)
  • DO NOT give it a imapact [sic], It might launch itself

As such, you'll be better off firing these little darts at inanimate objects without anyone else around. Maybe line up some cans, or maybe even 3D print some targets to ping things off of.

If you want to make your own, check out the schematics over on Maker World. The creator is also working on a gun that will fire these pellets, so keep an eye out for that, too.
